title: Respiratory alkalosis
body_html:
Definition
Respiratory alkalosis is a primary decrease in arterial carbon dioxide tension (PaCO₂) due to alveolar hyperventilation, leading to an increase in blood pH.

Causes
Hypoxaemia or hypoxia:
  • High altitude
  • Severe anaemia
  • Pulmonary embolism
  • Pneumonia
Central stimulation of respiration:
  • Anxiety, panic attacks
  • Pain, fever
  • Pregnancy
  • CNS disorders (stroke, subarachnoid haemorrhage, trauma, encephalitis)
  • Drugs (salicylates*, progesterone, catecholamines)
Iatrogenic or mechanical:
  • Overventilation (mechanical ventilation)

*salicylate overdose leads to a mixed respiratory alkalosis and metabolic acidosis. Early stimulation of the respiratory centre leads to a respiratory alkalosis whilst later the direct acid effects of salicylates (combined with acute renal failure) may lead to an acidosis

Pathophysiology
Hyperventilation causes excessive CO₂ excretion, leading to a fall in PaCO₂. This shifts the carbonic acid–bicarbonate equilibrium:

CO₂ + H₂O ↔ H₂CO₃ ↔ H⁺ + HCO₃⁻

A decrease in hydrogen ion concentration raises the pH (alkalosis).

Compensation:
  • Acute: limited renal compensation, small decrease in HCO₃⁻ (about 2 mmol/L for each 10 mmHg fall in PaCO₂)
  • Chronic (>2–3 days): kidneys excrete more bicarbonate, larger decrease in HCO₃⁻ (about 4–5 mmol/L per 10 mmHg fall in PaCO₂)

Clinical Features
  • Light-headedness, dizziness
  • Perioral and peripheral paresthesiae
  • Tetany, muscle cramps (due to decreased ionised calcium)
  • Confusion, syncope (in severe cases)
  • Chest tightness or dyspnoea (especially in anxiety-induced cases)

Arterial Blood Gas (ABG) Findings

ParameterAcuteChronic
pHIncreasedSlightly increased or near normal (partial compensation)
PaCO₂DecreasedDecreased
HCO₃⁻Slightly decreasedMore markedly decreased
